agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "Smart Route Optimization is a shift-start feature that assigns drivers an optimized delivery route from pending deliveries in their zone, with dynamic recalculation triggers and customer notification. It spans multiple bounded contexts: Route Planning, Fleet/Driver Management, Delivery Management, and Customer Notification.",
    "bounded_context": "Route Planning (primary), with dependencies on Fleet Management, Delivery Management, and Customer Notification",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "Optimized Route", "definition": "An ordered sequence of delivery stops assigned to a driver, calculated to minimize travel time/distance within a zone, respecting vehicle class constraints." },
      { "term": "Zone", "definition": "A geographic area that scopes both drivers and pending deliveries. A driver operates within a zone; deliveries belong to a zone." },
      { "term": "Pending Delivery", "definition": "A delivery that has not yet been assigned to a route or has been unassigned due to failure. It is eligible for route assignment." },
      { "term": "Shift", "definition": "A bounded work period for a driver. Route assignment is triggered at shift start." },
      { "term": "Licensed Vehicle Class", "definition": "A classification of the driver's vehicle (e.g., motorcycle, van, truck, freight) that constrains which deliveries they may carry based on size, weight, or cargo type." },
      { "term": "Route Recalculation", "definition": "The act of recomputing the optimized stop order in response to a triggering event (new delivery, failed delivery, manual deviation)." },
      { "term": "Next 3 Stops Window", "definition": "The set of the next three upcoming delivery stops on a driver's active route. Entry into this window triggers customer notification." },
      { "term": "Manual Deviation", "definition": "When a driver physically departs from the suggested route order — visiting stops out of sequence or traveling off-path." },
      { "term": "Failed Delivery", "definition": "A delivery attempt that was unsuccessful (recipient absent, access denied, etc.), removing it from the active route and triggering recalculation." },
      { "term": "Stop Time Estimate", "definition": "The expected duration a driver spends at each delivery stop. Defaults to 4 minutes but is overridable per zone by the operations team." },
      { "term": "Operations Team", "definition": "Internal stakeholders with authority to override configurable business parameters such as stop time estimates per zone." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "A driver MUST NOT be assigned a delivery whose cargo requirements exceed their licensed vehicle class capabilities.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Assigning a freight shipment to a motorcycle driver is physically impossible and likely illegal. Violating this corrupts the system's data integrity and creates real-world safety/legal liability. No business policy can override physics or law.",
      "testable_assertion": "For every delivery on a driver's route, delivery.requiredVehicleClass must be compatible with driver.licensedVehicleClass."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "A driver MUST only be assigned deliveries within their designated zone.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Zone assignment is a fundamental partitioning constraint. Cross-zone assignments break route optimization assumptions, insurance coverage boundaries, and operational accountability. The system's correctness depends on zone isolation.",
      "testable_assertion": "For every delivery on a driver's route, delivery.zone must equal driver.activeZone."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "Route assignment is triggered automatically when a driver starts their shift.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "The trigger timing (shift start) is a business policy. A future policy could allow pre-shift route preview or dispatcher-initiated assignment.",
      "testable_assertion": "When a driver's shift status transitions to 'active', an optimized route is computed and assigned within the same transactional boundary."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "The route MUST be recalculated when a new pending delivery is added to the driver's zone.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a responsiveness policy. Operations could decide to batch recalculations or defer them to avoid driver confusion. The trigger is a business decision.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a driver with an active route, when a new delivery enters their zone, the route is recalculated and the new delivery is included if eligible."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "The route MUST be recalculated when a delivery on the route is marked as failed.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Recalculation on failure is a policy to maintain route efficiency. A simpler policy could just skip the stop without full recalculation.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a driver with an active route, when a delivery is marked 'failed', it is removed and the remaining route is reoptimized."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "The route MUST be recalculated when the driver manually deviates from the suggested route.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Deviation-triggered recalculation is a UX/efficiency policy. Some systems allow drivers full autonomy without recalculation.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a driver with an active route, when the system detects the driver has deviated from the suggested stop order or path, a recalculation is triggered from the driver's current position."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "Customers MUST be notified when their delivery enters the 'next 3 stops' window.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "The notification threshold (3 stops) is a configurable business policy. The window size could change based on customer preferences or operational needs.",
      "testable_assertion": "When a route is calculated or recalculated, every customer whose delivery is within the next 3 stops receives a notification if they have not already been notified for this window entry."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-8",
      "rule": "The default estimated stop time is 4 minutes per delivery stop.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is an operational parameter, explicitly described as overridable by the operations team.",
      "testable_assertion": "When no zone-level override exists, route calculations use 4 minutes as the stop duration."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-9",
      "rule": "The operations team can override the stop time estimate on a per-zone basis.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is an administrative capability, a soft business policy about who can configure what.",
      "testable_assertion": "When an operations team member sets a custom stop time for a zone, all subsequent route calculations for that zone use the overridden value instead of the 4-minute default."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-10",
      "rule": "Only pending (unassigned, not yet delivered, not cancelled) deliveries are eligible for route assignment.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Assigning a completed or cancelled delivery to a route is data corruption — the driver would arrive at a stop with nothing to deliver. This is a state integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "Every delivery included in a newly computed route must have status 'pending' at the time of route calculation."
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [
    {
      "id": "AC-1",
      "given": "A driver starts their shift and there are pending deliveries in their zone compatible with their vehicle class",
      "when": "The shift is activated",
      "then": "An optimized route is automatically assigned containing only eligible deliveries, and the driver sees the ordered stop list"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-2",
      "given": "A driver has an active route",
      "when": "A new pending delivery is added to their zone that is compatible with their vehicle class",
      "then": "The route is recalculated to include the new delivery in the optimal position"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-3",
      "given": "A driver has an active route with delivery D at stop 5",
      "when": "Delivery D is marked as failed",
      "then": "Delivery D is removed from the route and the remaining stops are reoptimized"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-4",
      "given": "A driver has an active route with stops A→B→C→D",
      "when": "The driver skips B and goes directly to C",
      "then": "The route is recalculated from the driver's current position with remaining deliveries"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-5",
      "given": "A driver has an active route and completes a stop such that Customer X's delivery is now within the next 3 stops",
      "when": "The route position updates",
      "then": "Customer X receives a notification that their delivery is approaching"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-6",
      "given": "A motorcycle driver in Zone A",
      "when": "Route optimization runs and a freight shipment exists in Zone A",
      "then": "The freight shipment is excluded from the motorcycle driver's route"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-7",
      "given": "Zone A has a custom stop time override of 6 minutes set by operations",
      "when": "A route is calculated for a driver in Zone A",
      "then": "The route ETA uses 6 minutes per stop instead of the default 4 minutes"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-8",
      "given": "A driver starts their shift and there are zero pending deliveries in their zone",
      "when": "The shift is activated",
      "then": "The driver is assigned an empty route and is placed in an available/waiting state"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-9",
      "given": "A customer was already notified they are in the next-3-stops window",
      "when": "A route recalculation occurs but the customer remains within the next 3 stops",
      "then": "The customer is NOT sent a duplicate notification"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-10",
      "given": "A route recalculation occurs",
      "when": "A customer who was previously in the next-3-stops window is now outside it (pushed to stop 5)",
      "then": "The customer's ETA is updated but no 'you are no longer nearby' notification is required (spec is silent — see escalation)"
    }
  ],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Driver starts shift with no pending deliveries in zone", "expected_behavior": "Driver enters an idle/available state with an empty route. When a delivery arrives in their zone, route is calculated and assigned." },
    { "scenario": "All pending deliveries in a zone require a vehicle class no active driver possesses", "expected_behavior": "Deliveries remain unassigned. System should alert operations that deliveries are unroutable due to vehicle class mismatch." },
    { "scenario": "Route recalculation triggered while a previous recalculation is still in progress", "expected_behavior": "The system must handle concurrent recalculation triggers — either queue them or cancel-and-restart. Two recalculations must not produce two conflicting active routes." },
    { "scenario": "Multiple drivers in the same zone start shifts simultaneously", "expected_behavior": "Pending deliveries must be partitioned across drivers — a single delivery must not appear on multiple drivers' routes. This is a critical consistency constraint." },
    { "scenario": "Driver deviates but returns to the original route order", "expected_behavior": "Deviation detection should distinguish between a true off-path deviation and a temporary GPS drift or minor detour. Spurious recalculations degrade driver experience." },
    { "scenario": "A delivery is added to a zone and immediately cancelled before recalculation completes", "expected_behavior": "The recalculated route must not include the cancelled delivery. Route calculation must read delivery status at computation time, not trigger time." },
    { "scenario": "Customer's delivery oscillates in and out of the 3-stop window due to rapid recalculations", "expected_behavior": "Notification deduplication must prevent notification spam. Once notified, the customer should not be re-notified unless delivery drops out and re-enters the window after a meaningful interval." },
    { "scenario": "Driver's only remaining delivery is marked failed", "expected_behavior": "Route becomes empty. Driver returns to idle/available state or is directed to return to depot, depending on operational policy." },
    { "scenario": "Operations team sets stop time to 0 or a negative value", "expected_behavior": "System must validate stop time overrides: must be a positive duration. Zero or negative values are rejected." },
    { "scenario": "A new delivery arrives in the zone but the driver is currently at a stop (mid-delivery)", "expected_behavior": "Recalculation should not interrupt the current stop. The new route takes effect after the current stop is completed." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Route optimization directly reduces fuel costs, increases deliveries per shift (throughput), and reduces overtime. Incorrect vehicle class matching could result in failed deliveries, wasted trips, and potential regulatory fines. Stop time accuracy affects ETA reliability which impacts customer retention.",
    "operational": "Dynamic recalculation increases system complexity and compute load. Operations team needs tooling to manage zone-level overrides. Deviation detection requires real-time GPS tracking infrastructure. Concurrent multi-driver route partitioning is a hard distributed systems problem.",
    "user": "Drivers get clear, optimized routes reducing cognitive load. Customers get proactive proximity notifications improving satisfaction and reducing 'where is my delivery' support calls. However, excessive recalculations could frustrate drivers with constantly changing routes.",
    "risk": "Vehicle class violation is the highest-risk failure — legal liability and safety. Notification spam from oscillating 3-stop windows damages customer trust. Race conditions in multi-driver delivery assignment could cause duplicate deliveries or missed deliveries. Deviation detection false positives cause unnecessary recalculations.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Drivers", "impact": "Primary users — receive optimized routes, experience recalculations mid-shift. UX must balance optimization with route stability." },
      { "group": "Customers", "impact": "Receive proximity notifications. ETA accuracy depends on stop time estimates. Notification quality affects brand perception." },
      { "group": "Operations Team", "impact": "Gains zone-level configuration control. Needs visibility into unroutable deliveries and vehicle class mismatches." },
      { "group": "Dispatchers", "impact": "Spec is silent on dispatcher role — are they fully replaced by automation or do they retain override capability? This is a significant gap." },
      { "group": "Finance/Management", "impact": "Expects measurable improvement in deliveries-per-shift and fuel cost reduction. Needs reporting on route efficiency metrics." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 55,
    "reasoning": "The spec covers the happy path well but has significant gaps in multi-driver scenarios, deviation detection criteria, notification lifecycle, and dispatcher authority. The bounded context boundaries are implied but not explicit — vehicle class matching could live in Fleet Management or Route Planning. Several edge cases require policy decisions not present in the spec.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Vehicle class constraint is a hard invariant",
      "Zone scoping for delivery assignment",
      "Shift-start triggers route assignment",
      "Three recalculation triggers are clearly defined",
      "Stop time default and override mechanism"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "How deliveries are partitioned across multiple drivers in the same zone",
      "What constitutes a 'manual deviation' — GPS threshold? Skipping a stop? Visiting out of order?",
      "Notification lifecycle — what happens when a customer leaves the 3-stop window after being notified?",
      "Whether dispatchers retain any manual override authority",
      "How recalculation interacts with in-progress stops",
      "Whether route recalculation for new deliveries applies to ALL drivers in the zone or only the nearest/most optimal driver"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "Each driver is assigned to exactly one zone per shift",
      "Vehicle class compatibility is a binary check (compatible or not), not a ranking",
      "Route optimization means minimizing travel time/distance, not other objectives like priority or SLA",
      "Notification is a one-way push (SMS/push notification), not requiring customer acknowledgment",
      "A delivery belongs to exactly one zone and cannot span zones",
      "Failed deliveries are removed from the route entirely, not re-queued for retry on the same route"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The spec does not define how pending deliveries are partitioned when multiple drivers are active in the same zone. Is it first-come-first-served at shift start? Nearest driver? Balanced load? This is a critical design decision that affects fairness, efficiency, and delivery guarantees.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers", "Operations Team", "Customers"],
      "options": [
        "A: Assign all pending deliveries to the first driver who starts their shift, subsequent drivers get only new arrivals",
        "B: Partition deliveries across all active drivers using a load-balancing algorithm at each recalculation",
        "C: Let dispatchers manually partition, with optimization only within each driver's assigned set"
      ],
      "recommendation": "B — load-balanced partitioning with recalculation. This maximizes throughput and is standard in fleet logistics. But it requires cross-driver coordination which adds complexity."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "What constitutes a 'manual deviation'? The spec says recalculate when a driver 'deviates from the suggested route' but does not define deviation criteria. Options: visiting stops out of order, traveling a different road to the same next stop, exceeding a distance threshold from the planned path, or explicitly skipping a stop.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers", "Operations Team"],
      "options": [
        "A: Deviation = driver completes a stop out of the suggested sequence",
        "B: Deviation = driver's GPS position exceeds N meters from the planned path for M minutes",
        "C: Both A and B trigger recalculation"
      ],
      "recommendation": "A — sequence-based deviation is deterministic, simple to implement, and avoids GPS noise false positives. Path-based deviation (B) can be added later as an enhancement."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "When a new delivery arrives in a zone with multiple active drivers, which driver's route gets recalculated to include it? All of them? Only the nearest? The one with the lightest load?",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers", "Customers", "Operations Team"],
      "options": [
        "A: Recalculate all drivers' routes in the zone holistically",
        "B: Assign to the driver whose route would be least disrupted (nearest to new delivery's location)",
        "C: Assign to the driver with the fewest remaining stops"
      ],
      "recommendation": "A — holistic recalculation is the most correct but most expensive. Start with B for MVP and evolve to A."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "The spec does not mention the dispatcher role at all. In most logistics operations, dispatchers have override authority to manually reassign deliveries, lock routes, or force-add stops. Is this system fully automated with no dispatcher intervention, or do dispatchers retain override capability?",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Dispatchers", "Operations Team", "Drivers"],
      "options": [
        "A: Fully automated — no dispatcher intervention possible",
        "B: Automated with dispatcher override capability (can lock stops, reassign, force routes)",
        "C: Dispatcher-assisted — system suggests, dispatcher approves"
      ],
      "recommendation": "B — full automation with override capability. Pure automation without human escape hatches fails in edge cases that algorithms cannot anticipate (road closures, customer special requests, driver emergencies)."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The notification behavior when a customer drops OUT of the 3-stop window after recalculation is undefined. If a customer was told 'your delivery is 3 stops away' and then a recalculation pushes them to stop 7, the original notification becomes misleading.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers"],
      "options": [
        "A: Send an updated ETA notification when customer drops out of the window",
        "B: Do nothing — the original notification stands, customer will receive a new one when they re-enter the window",
        "C: Send an apology/update notification explaining the delay"
      ],
      "recommendation": "A — send an updated ETA. Silent ETA changes erode customer trust. This is a customer experience decision that product must own."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
